title: Real or Rendered: Spot the Fake

modules.title: When Seeing Stopped Meaning Believing
modules.sub: Discover how AI broke the rules of visual evidence
modules.description: Learners step into the role of a junior investigator and explore how AI-generated media has made it harder to trust photos, audio, and text at face value. They examine real cases where fake content caused genuine harm, and begin to understand why a reliable detection process matters.

modules.title: The Tell-Tale Pixels: Finding Clues
modules.sub: Train your eye to catch what AI gets wrong
modules.description: Learners analyse a set of suspect images, audio clips, and text samples, hunting for the telltale glitches and patterns that AI tools leave behind — odd hands, unnatural lighting, rhythm errors in speech, and repetitive phrasing. They make judgment calls on whether each piece of content is real or rendered, then see how their decisions affect what the fictional news agency publishes. This hands-on investigation builds a practical sense of what to look for before any formal checklist exists.

modules.title: Who Made This and Why?
modules.sub: Follow the motive trail behind AI-generated content
modules.description: Learners shift from spotting technical flaws to asking bigger questions: who would create this content, and what do they gain from it being believed? They work through a series of short scenarios where they must weigh up source credibility, platform context, and likely intent before deciding whether to flag a piece. This module introduces the idea that verification is not just a technical skill but a reasoning process about people and power.

modules.title: Build Your Own Fake-Detector Checklist
modules.sub: Turn your investigation skills into a reusable tool
modules.description: Learners synthesise everything from the previous modules into a personal verification checklist covering images, audio, and written content. They test their checklist against a final batch of unseen examples and then explain their reasoning aloud or in writing, as if briefing a colleague. The finished checklist is something they can take away and apply whenever they encounter content that feels uncertain.

synopsis: You're a junior investigator at a news agency where AI-generated images, voices, and text keep slipping through — and it's your job to catch them before they go public. Every choice you make changes what gets published and who gets hurt.
rationale: As AI-generated content floods social feeds, students this age are primary targets of synthetic misinformation yet receive almost no structured practice in detection before they encounter it.
learning_outcome: Students can apply a repeatable verification checklist to images, audio, and written content to judge whether it was likely AI-generated, and explain their reasoning to someone else.
source_signals: Modern-Day Oracles or Bullshit Machines? How to thrive in a ChatGPT world
Tali Horowitz: The Key Issue with AI Chatbots and Teens
Digital Citizenship: Teaching Teens to Navigate the online World Responsibly | BWWT Ep. 240
nearest_existing: Every New Medium Rewrites What We Believe
